summary: POD GGUF BLOCKER diagnosed (screenshot, live 5090 Pod): ComfyUI-GGUF node import FAILS on the Pod with `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'gguf'`. TWO gaps: (1) the Pod image's node_lock.json (mpi-ci/cubric-vision-pod/node_lock.json) is a STALE COPY — it does NOT have the ComfyUI-GGUF entry I added to the app's dev_configs/node_lock.json, so the image build never installed the node OR its gguf pip dep (per the bump-rebuild law: node_lock edit = rebuild BOTH images). (2) The per-model VOLUME-install path (routes/remoteModels.js remoteInstallDep, line 284) only passes install_command to the wrapper when dep.installRequirementsCommand (a CUSTOM command) is set — it SILENTLY DROPS installRequirements:true (the standard requirements.txt flag). ComfyUI-GGUF uses installRequirements:true → so even though the wrapper pushed the node folder to the volume, it never ran `pip install -r requirements.txt` → no gguf pkg → import fails. FIX OPTIONS: (a) add ComfyUI-GGUF to the Pod node_lock.json + rebuild the Pod image (bakes node+gguf in, but ComfyUI-GGUF is MODEL-specific not universal — does it belong baked? the image loop installs ALL node_lock nodes regardless of installOnEngine, so adding it there WOULD bake it = acceptable for now); AND/OR (b) FIX remoteModels.js so installRequirements:true also triggers a remote pip install -r requirements.txt (the wrapper must support running requirements.txt for a volume node, not just a custom command) — this is the GENERAL fix so future model-specific nodes with a requirements.txt work remotely. (b) is the real bug. Detail in handoff 60c42614.

VRAM RESEARCH CLOSED (docs/krea2/resolution.md). USER ASKED: users claim Krea2 runs on 8GB VRAM - is it real? ANSWER: conditional and MIS-LABELED in most cases. NO first-party hardware spec exists anywhere (krea-ai GitHub, HF model card, ComfyUI docs tutorial, krea.ai technical report all publish ZERO VRAM requirements - anyone citing an official 8GB figure is citing nothing). The 8GB claims resolve to THREE different things, only ONE of which is our path: (1) community GGUF Q4 ~7.2-7.7GiB, fits fully resident, MOST LIKELY REFERENT, needs a community-forked loader (city96 throws `Unexpected architecture type`) - WE DO NOT SHIP GGUF (MPI-190 removed it), so this path does not exist in Cubric Vision; (2) fp8_scaled + ComfyUI DynamicVRAM RAM-spilling = WHAT WE SHIP, two confirmed reports (RTX A2000, RTX 3060 Ti), works but needs ~24GB system RAM and est ~31s/img on a 3060 Ti; (3) official NVFP4 7.67GiB, fits 8GB but is BLACKWELL-ONLY (RTX 50xx hardware format). COMFORTABLE PRACTICAL MINIMUM = 16GB VRAM (RTX 5080 confirmed, ~3.5GB headroom). CRITICAL RECONCILIATION - our computed table is CORRECT, no tweak needed: footprint.js yields floor 8GB (MIN_FLOOR clamp, since 0.25*22.10=5.5<8) with rows 8=>16GB RAM, 16=>8, 24=>0 resident. Research measured PEAK SAMPLING VRAM = 12.40GB; our transformer alone is 12.24GB. They MATCH because ComfyUI EVICTS the Qwen3-VL-4B text encoder (4.88GB) BEFORE the sampler loads - encoder and transformer are NEVER both resident. So the text encoder is NOT an OOM culprit (zero reports attribute an OOM to it). And the apparent gap between our 16GB RAM row and the researchs ~24GB is NOT a contradiction: footprint.js states MODEL need, not SYSTEM total (its header: OS reserve ~10-20GB is the users own headroom, surfaced as a footnote, never baked in). 16GB model + OS reserve ~= 24GB observed. CONSISTENT. => DO NOT special-case footprint.js for Krea2; the formula independently lands on the same 8GB floor the users report. SPEED REFERENCE (RTX 3090, 1024^2, 8 steps): fp8_scaled = 14.82 s/img; int8_convrot = 7.70 s/img (1.92x faster via Ampere tensor cores) - a future quant lever. NO 4090 timing exists anywhere. AMD ROCm is BROKEN (kernel crash at step 1) - relevant to the portable Linux build. UI COPY GUIDANCE: quote 16GB as the comfortable minimum; 8GB is the honest floor with a real RAM cost.

Color shift LOCATED (DaVinci): crop-export (our ffmpeg pipeline) == source, NO shift. So ffmpeg/export is clean; extend-from-last-frame drift is NOT export. Shift is DECODE-SIDE ONLY: mediabunny CanvasSink (WebCodecs) renders more vivid than true frame; <video> element is correct. Fix target = make frameSink canvas color-match <video> (range/matrix). Awaiting research agent.

Color shift ROOT CAUSE: decoded VideoFrame reports CORRECT color (bt709/bt709/bt709 primaries/transfer/matrix, fullRange:false = limited). Shift is in Chromium drawImage(VideoFrame)->2D-canvas step: it expands limited-range to full-range RGB (sink vivid), while <video>/DaVinci render limited-range as-is (correct). Both technically valid but disagree; <video> is our reference. CanvasSink has NO color option. Fix = correct range in draw. Research agent still finding exact minimal remedy.

Color fix approach: range expansion is INSIDE WebCodecs decode (all 4 draw paths incl copyTo-RGBA give identical vivid pixels; VideoFrame pixels already full-range). Not a draw-trick fix. Simple luma-range compress (219/255) fits R only, not G/B - transform is per-pixel YUV-space, mean-fitting over-corrects. Need proper matrix from research agent. User chose: fix in frameSink. HOLDING for research.

Color fix ANALYSIS: per-channel pixel fit sink->video = R(a0.976,b-2.3) G(a1.016,b-7.4) B(a1.0,b0,resid0). Blue IDENTICAL, R/G diverge by different slopes = YUV MATRIX mismatch (BT.601 vs BT.709), NOT a range op. Confirms research bug 40539111. copyTo-RGBA disproven (same vivid pixels). EXACT fix = self-convert YUV->RGB with correct bt709 limited matrix from copyTo I420 planes. Per-channel RGB approx = resid3/maxDev15 (imperfect). Scope escalation - flagged to user.
